A1s:
The faster version replaces the per-element `indexOf` scan with a `Set`, dropping it from O(nÂ²) to O(n):

```javascript
function dedup(a){ return [...new Set(a)]; }
```

Both preserve first-seen order. The improvement: each `indexOf` call is a linear scan, so the original does ~nÂ²/2 comparisons (e.g. ~50 billion for n=100k, effectively unrunnable), while the `Set` version does a single O(n) pass with O(1) average lookups â€” roughly an n-fold speedup that grows with input size.

One behavioral difference worth knowing: the original silently drops every `NaN` (because `indexOf` can't match `NaN`), whereas the `Set` version keeps a single `NaN`. The `Set` behavior is almost always what you actually want, but if some downstream code relied on the old quirk, that's the one thing to check.
